sanskrit_devanagari:
जरामरणमोक्षाय मामाश्रित्य यतन्ति ये ।
ते ब्रह्म तद्विदु: कृत्स्नमध्यात्मं कर्म चाखिलम् ॥ २९ ॥
sanskrit_iast: jarā-maraṇa-mokṣāyamām āśritya yatanti yete brahma tad viduḥ kṛtsnamadhyātmaṁ karma cākhilam

translations.verbatim_quote: Intelligent persons who are endeavoring for liberation from old age and death take refuge in Me in devotional service. They are actually Brahman because they entirely know everything about transcendental activities.

Intelligent persons who are endeavoring for liberation from old age and death take refuge in Me in devotional service. They are actually Brahman because they entirely know everything about transcendental activities.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1670 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Birth, death, old age and diseases affect this material body, but not the spiritual body. There is no birth, death, old age and disease for the spiritual body, so one who attains a spiritual body, becomes one of the associates of the Supreme Personality of Godhead and engages in eternal devotional s

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.29 Ye, those who; yatanti, strive; asritya, by resorting; mam, to Me, the supreme God, by having their minds absorbed in Me; jara-marana-moksaya, for becoming free from old age and death; te, they; viduh, know; tat, that; brahma, Brahman, which is the Supreme; they know krtsnam, everythi
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-7-29-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Those who, by resorting (āśritya) to Krishna and absorbing their minds in him, strive (yatanti) for becoming free from old-age-and-death (jarā-maraṇa-mokṣa) — they know (viduḥ) that brahman, the supreme; they know everything (kṛtsnam) about adhyātma, the individual Self (the indwelling entity); and they know all (akhilam) about karma (action).

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.29 Those who take refuge in Me and strive for deliverance from old age and death, i.e., for the vision of the real nature of the self as distinct from the Prakrti, they know brahman (the pure individual self). They also know all about the individual self, and the whole of Karma.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-7-29-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Those who take refuge in Krishna and strive for deliverance from old age and death — that is, for the vision of the real nature of the self as distinct from prakṛti — they know brahman (the pure individual self), the whole of the individual self (adhyātma), and the whole of karma. This attainment is kaivalya — the aloofness of the self in absorption in its own bliss.

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Shankara reads brahman as the supreme reality (Krishna as the absolute) and adhyātma as the indwelling self that is non-different from brahman; Ramanuja reads brahman here in a narrower sense as the pure individual self (kaivalya), with adhyātma as the individual self distinct from prakṛti — and uses 7.30 to mark a separate higher attainment for those who know Krishna in adhibhūta/adhidaiva/adhiyajña. The engineering analog is heavily STRETCHED — the verse's reach is metaphysical and cosmological. Forward-pointer to Ch 8, where adhyātma/adhibhūta/adhidaiva/adhiyajña are typologized.

literal_meaning: Those who strive for liberation from old age and death, taking refuge in me — they know that brahman entirely, the adhyātma, all karma.
traditional_meaning_consensus: jarā-maraṇa-mokṣāya mām āśritya yatanti ye / te brahma tad viduḥ kṛtsnam adhyātmaṁ karma cākhilam — those who, taking refuge in Krishna, strive (yatanti) for liberation (mokṣa) from old age (jarā) and death (maraṇa) — they know that brahman entirely (kṛtsnam), the adhyātma (the individual Self / the embodied substrate), and all karma (action) entirely. Shankara: those striving for jarā-maraṇa-mokṣa with their minds absorbed in Krishna know brahman, the supreme; they know everything about adhyātma (the indwelling entity) and all about karma. Ramanuja: those striving for the vision of the self as distinct from prakṛti, taking refuge in Krishna, know brahman (the pure individual self), the adhyātma (individual self), and the whole of karma — the kaivalya goal.

remaining_gaps: Madhva, Abhinavagupta, Sridhara Swami, Kesava Kashmiri commentaries available in raw/ but not pulled into the source pack.
